title: Introduction to dashes
description: Dashes indicate interruptions, set off asides, and show interruptions in speech. Learn the different functions of dashes in this exercise.
topic_path: Punctuation: the colon, semicolon, and more

assessment_items.question_content:
**Where should the dash(es) go in this sentence?**

>I went to Seattle my favorite city for vacation this year.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Dashes** introduce an *interruption* in thought. They have a few important uses:

* A pair of dashes can enclose extra information or commentary, much like parentheses.

 *The cat—a long-haired calico—basked in the sunlight.*

* A single dash can introduce an explanation or addition, much like a colon.

 *They stared in disbelief—the man had turned into a wolf!*

* A dash can be used to indicate a break or interruption in dialogue.

 *"Please—" started Alice, before the teacher cut her off.*

"My favorite city" is extra information that isn't grammatically necessary, so we should enclose it with dashes:

>**I went to Seattle—my favorite city—for vacation this year.**

choices.content: I went to Seattle—my favorite city—for vacation this year.
I went to Seattle my favorite—city for vacation this year.
I went—to Seattle my favorite city—for vacation this year.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Where should the dash(es) go in this sentence?**

>“But I thought I told you” Alex yelled before her brother interrupted her.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Dashes** introduce an *interruption* in thought. They have a few important uses:

* A pair of dashes can enclose extra information or commentary, much like parentheses.

 *The cat—a long-haired calico—basked in the sunlight.*

* A single dash can introduce an explanation or addition, much like a colon.

 *They stared in disbelief—the man had turned into a wolf!*

* A dash can be used to indicate a break or interruption in dialogue.

 *"Please—" started Alice, before the teacher cut her off.*

The dialogue in this sentence is interrupted, so we should end it with a dash:

>**“But I thought I told you—” Alex yelled before her brother interrupted her.**

choices.content: “But I thought—I told you” Alex yelled before her brother interrupted her.
“But I thought I told you—” Alex yelled before her brother interrupted her.
“But I thought I told you” Alex yelled before her brother—interrupted her.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Where should the dash(es) go in this sentence?**

>If you think about it really think about it something doesn’t make sense.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Dashes** introduce an *interruption* in thought. They have a few important uses:

* A pair of dashes can enclose extra information or commentary, much like parentheses.

 *The cat—a long-haired calico—basked in the sunlight.*

* A single dash can introduce an explanation or addition, much like a colon.

 *They stared in disbelief—the man had turned into a wolf!*

* A dash can be used to indicate a break or interruption in dialogue.

 *"Please—" started Alice, before the teacher cut her off.*

"Really think about it" is extra commentary that isn't grammatically necessary, so we should enclose it with dashes:

>**If you think about it—really think about it—something doesn’t make sense.
**

choices.content: If you think—about it really think—about it something doesn’t make sense.
If you think about it—really think about it—something doesn’t make sense.
If you think about it really think about it—something doesn’t make sense.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Where should the dash(es) go in this sentence?**

>I bought three things at The Mountain Store snow boots, skis, and a parka.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Dashes** introduce an *interruption* in thought. They have a few important uses:

* A pair of dashes can enclose extra information or commentary, much like parentheses.

 *The cat—a long-haired calico—basked in the sunlight.*

* A single dash can introduce an explanation or addition, much like a colon.

 *They stared in disbelief—the man had turned into a wolf!*

* A dash can be used to indicate a break or interruption in dialogue.

 *"Please—" started Alice, before the teacher cut her off.*

"Snow boots, skis, and a parka" is an explanation added to the end of the sentence, so we should introduce it with a dash:

>**I bought three things at The Mountain Store—snow boots, skis, and a parka.**

choices.content: I bought three things—at The Mountain Store—snow boots, skis, and a parka.
I bought three things—at The Mountain Store snow boots, skis, and a parka.
I bought three things at The Mountain Store—snow boots, skis, and a parka.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Where should the dash(es) go in this sentence?**

>Lucy the girl sitting over there eating an ice cream cone is my best friend.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:

* A pair of dashes can enclose extra information or commentary, much like parentheses.

 *The cat—a long-haired calico—basked in the sunlight.*

* A single dash can introduce an explanation or addition, much like a colon.

 *They stared in disbelief—the man had turned into a wolf!*

* A dash can be used to indicate a break or interruption in dialogue.

 *"Please—" started Alice, before the teacher cut her off.*

"The girl sitting over there eating an ice cream cone" is extra information that isn't grammatically necessary, so we should enclose it with dashes:

>**Lucy—the girl sitting over there eating an ice cream cone—is my best friend.
**

choices.content: Lucy—the girl sitting over there eating an ice cream cone—is my best friend.
Lucy—the girl sitting over there eating an ice cream cone is my best friend.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Where should the dash(es) go in this sentence?**

>Lake Tahoe a body of water on the border of California and Nevada is a popular spot for tourists.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Dashes** introduce an *interruption* in thought. They have a few important uses:

* A pair of dashes can enclose extra information or commentary, much like parentheses.

 *The cat—a long-haired calico—basked in the sunlight.*

* A single dash can introduce an explanation or addition, much like a colon.

 *They stared in disbelief—the man had turned into a wolf!*

* A dash can be used to indicate a break or interruption in dialogue.

 *"Please—" started Alice, before the teacher cut her off.*

"A body of water on the border of California and Nevada" is extra information that isn't grammatically necessary, so we should enclose it with dashes:

>**Lake Tahoe—a body of water on the border of California and Nevada—is a popular spot for tourists.
**

choices.content: Lake Tahoe—a body of water on the border of California and Nevada—is a popular spot for tourists.
Lake Tahoe—a body of water—on the border of California and Nevada is a popular spot for tourists.
Lake Tahoe a body of water on the border—of California and Nevada—is a popular spot for tourists.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Where should the dash(es) go in this sentence?**

>I only had one thing on my mind this Halloween candy corn.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Dashes** introduce an *interruption* in thought. They have a few important uses:

* A pair of dashes can enclose extra information or commentary, much like parentheses.

 *The cat—a long-haired calico—basked in the sunlight.*

 *They stared in disbelief—the man had turned into a wolf!*

* A dash can be used to indicate a break or interruption in dialogue.

 *"Please—" started Alice, before the teacher cut her off.*

"Candy corn" is an explanation added to the end of the sentence, so we should introduce it with a dash:

>**I only had one thing on my mind this Halloween—candy corn.**

choices.content: I only had one thing on my mind this—Halloween—candy corn.
I only had one thing on my mind this Halloween—candy corn.
I only had one thing on—my mind this Halloween candy corn.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the dash being used for in this sentence?**

>“No, I haven’t seen a hissing cockroach before—waah! Is that what they look like?” said Mia, watching the little critter clamber up the wall of its enclosure.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Dashes** introduce an *interruption* in thought. They have a few important uses:

* A pair of dashes can enclose extra information or commentary, much like parentheses.

 *The cat—a long-haired calico—basked in the sunlight.*

* A single dash can introduce an explanation or addition, much like a colon.

 *They stared in disbelief—the man had turned into a wolf!*

* A dash can be used to indicate a break or interruption in dialogue.

 *"Please—" started Alice, before the teacher cut her off.*
The dash in this sentence **shows an interruption in dialogue.**

choices.content: It's being used to show an interruption in dialogue.
It's introducing a list of items.
